Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside time on weekends and a few evenings, but with everything going on financially and working part-time, it rarely went as planned. I'm a visual learner, so I'd make colorful notes and diagrams when I could, but finding consistent time was really hard. I probably averaged around 16 hours a week when I could manage it, but those hours were scattered and often late at night.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what shifts I had at work. Some weeks I'd barely touch my coursework because I was covering extra hours to pay rent, then I'd have to cram everything before deadlines. I wanted to be more regular about it, but life just kept getting in the way. The anxiety of knowing I wasn't keeping up made it even harder to start sometimes.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or realizing I'd missed something important. Sometimes a classmate from my peer support group would message me asking about an assignment, and that would remind me to check the LMS. I also logged in when I got notification emails, though I have to admit sometimes I'd see the notification and feel too overwhelmed to actually open it right away.
Q4: For a little while in the middle of the semester, checking the LMS became more routine after I connected with my peer support group. We'd coordinate our study sessions and remind each other about things, so I'd log in to check discussion posts or see what materials they were referencing. But honestly, that routine broke down when my stress levels peaked and I started avoiding everything that made me anxious.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful for me because I learn best by seeing things explained visually. I watched over four hours of video content because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the concepts at my own pace. The diagrams and visual explanations stuck with me way better than reading dense text. When I actually had time to engage with them properly, they made a real difference.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and some of the supplementary resources. Honestly, reading long documents on a screen is hard for me, and I couldn't always afford to print things out. The discussion forums also felt intimidating—I only posted three times because I was scared of saying something wrong in front of everyone. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it directly connected to what would be on exams or assignments. With limited hours and so much anxiety about falling behind, I had to prioritize ruthlessly. If something helped me understand a concept visually or if my peer group said it was essential, I'd make time for it. Otherwise, it felt like a luxury I couldn't afford.
Q8: Optional activities felt like nice-to-haves that I couldn't justify when I was already drowning with required work. There was always this guilt about not doing them, but required assignments had deadlines and consequences, so they won every time. Sometimes the optional stuff actually looked interesting, but my brain would just say 'you can't even keep up with the basics, don't add more to your plate.'
Q9: Absolutely. Once I watched a video or downloaded a PDF, I'd often study from my notes offline because I don't always have reliable internet at home. I made handwritten summaries with diagrams and color-coding that I'd review on the bus to work or during breaks. A lot of my actual learning happened with my notebook and colored pens, not logged into anything.
Q10: So many times. I'd download lecture slides once, then spend hours redrawing concepts as visual maps in my notebook. My peer study group would meet at the library and we'd work through problems together on whiteboards—none of that showed up anywhere. Some of my best understanding came from explaining things to others in person, which obviously the LMS couldn't capture.
Q11: Yes, constantly. With my limited data plan and unreliable wifi, I learned to download everything I could in one session and then work offline for days. I'd watch a video once online, take detailed visual notes, and then review those notes repeatedly without ever logging back in. The LMS would show one view, but I'd have studied that content five or six times from my notes.
Q12: There were several really dark weeks when my anxiety was through the roof and I just couldn't face anything academic. I was scared about money, scared about failing, scared about everything. During those times, even opening my laptop felt impossible. My attendance dropped because getting to campus meant spending money on transport that I needed for food. The fear was paralyzing.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work shifts or stressed about bills, the LMS became this symbol of all my failures and fears. I'd avoid it because seeing incomplete assignments made my anxiety spike. Fatigue made it hard to concentrate even when I did log in—I'd stare at videos without really processing anything. My competing responsibilities constantly pulled me away from coursework.
Q14: Many times I'd log in and just scroll through materials feeling completely disconnected. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be racing about other problems—whether I could make rent, whether I was going to fail. Sometimes I'd click through pages just to feel like I was doing something, but nothing was actually sinking in. Those sessions felt hollow and frustrating.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they were important. The fear of not doing them well enough made me avoid starting them at all. It's this terrible cycle where anxiety makes you procrastinate, and procrastinating makes you more anxious. I knew my assignment completion rate was awful, but facing that reality felt worse than avoiding it in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me to engage with the LMS consistently. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, trying to cram weeks of content into hours. It wasn't healthy or effective, but the pressure of a deadline could cut through my avoidance in a way nothing else could. My whole engagement pattern was deadline-driven chaos.
Q17: When I got good feedback on the assignments I did complete—like my assignment scores were actually really high when I submitted—it gave me a boost of confidence. But honestly, feedback often came too late to change my behavior for the next thing. And sometimes positive feedback made me feel worse because it highlighted how much potential I was wasting by not engaging more consistently.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with seven online courses to juggle simultaneously. Having so many things to track across different modules made it hard to prioritize. I wished there was more scaffolding or check-ins to help me stay on track. The flexibility was supposed to help, but for someone like me with high anxiety, it just meant more opportunities to fall behind.
Q19: The visual resources and video content made engagement easier when I could access them. But the sheer volume of materials and the lack of clear prioritization made it harder. I never knew what was truly essential versus supplementary. Also, the discussion requirements felt intimidating for someone already scared of judgment. More structured, smaller milestones would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I'd log in frequently to check things, watch videos, look at materials—but then freeze when it came to actually submitting work. The fear of not being good enough, of being judged, made the final step of submission terrifying. You can engage with content without having the courage or energy to produce something from it.
Q21: That resonates with me too, actually. My exam score was decent and I passed despite my low visible engagement because I was doing a lot of learning offline. The LMS numbers don't show my notebook full of diagrams, my study sessions with peers, or the hours I spent reviewing downloaded materials. Some of us just learn in ways that don't leave digital footprints.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I'm actually understanding something or just going through motions. They definitely miss all the offline work, the emotional struggles, the peer conversations, and the real reasons behind irregular patterns. Numbers don't capture fear or determination.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and feeling connected to the learning process, not just completing tasks. For me, it's when I'm genuinely curious and making those visual connections in my mind, when concepts click into place. It's also about feeling like part of a learning community, which my peer support group gave me even when I couldn't face the LMS.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It can't show the tears, the panic attacks, the nights I stayed up studying from handwritten notes because I couldn't afford internet. It can't show that my resilience kept me going when everything said I should quit. My low numbers hide a massive internal battle that I'm still winning, one day at a time.
